questionText: When students don’t understand a topic, what do you usually think first?
opt0_text: I need to explain it more clearly
opt1_text: Which students are struggling and why
opt3_text: What activity might help them grasp it

questionText: When a lesson doesn't work as expected, you are most likely to:
opt0_text: Reflect on what you could change next time
opt1_text: Discuss it with another teacher
opt2_text: Stick to the plan and move ahead
opt3_text: Check student work to see what went wrong

questionText: When two students learn at very different speeds, what do you usually do?
opt0_text: Continue teaching at the same pace for everyone
opt1_text: Give extra homework to the slower learner
opt2_text: Provide varied support so both can progress
opt3_text: Ask the faster learner to help the slower one

questionText: How do you understand a child’s strengths or interests?
opt1_text: By observing classroom behaviour and interactions
opt3_text: I don’t specifically track strengths or interests

questionText: How do you ensure shy students participate?
opt0_text: Call them out randomly so they answer
opt1_text: Pair them with confident students
opt2_text: Create low-pressure activities where everyone contributes
opt3_text: Let them stay quiet—they will talk when ready

questionText: Before starting a new chapter, how do you check learning levels?
opt1_text: By giving a quick informal check (questions/activity)
opt2_text: By checking the previous exam scores
opt3_text: By asking students if they already know the topic

questionText: When a child performs poorly, how do you find the cause?
opt1_text: Re-check the question paper
opt2_text: Use small diagnostic tasks to find exact gaps
opt3_text: Talk to the parent first

questionText: How often do you assess progress during teaching?
opt1_text: Only when I feel students are slow
opt3_text: Daily quick checks (exit slips, oral checks, tasks)

questionText: What guides your lesson planning the most?
opt1_text: Ensuring students remember key facts
opt3_text: Following what other teachers do

questionText: How do you check if students can apply their learning?
opt0_text: By asking them to memorise definitions
opt1_text: By giving tasks that require real-life application
opt3_text: By checking notebook completeness

questionText: Which activity do you prefer to build competencies?
opt1_text: Worksheets focused on recall
opt3_text: Dictation and repetition

questionText: When did you last try a new teaching method or tool?
opt1_text: Within the last year
opt3_text: This week or last week

questionText: How do you view feedback on your teaching?
opt1_text: It is acceptable but not necessary
opt3_text: I actively seek it

questionText: How do you stay updated about best practices?
opt1_text: I follow what senior teachers do
opt3_text: I proactively learn from courses, reading, or peers

questionText: How often do you communicate learning progress to parents (not just marks)?
opt1_text: Once every few months
opt3_text: Regular short updates (weekly/fortnightly)

questionText: What do you usually discuss with parents?
opt1_text: Mostly marks and homework
opt3_text: Specific strategies to support learning at home

questionText: How do you involve parents in the child’s learning?
opt1_text: Ask them to ensure homework is done
opt2_text: Share simple activities to do at home
opt3_text: Work with them as partners, discussing progress and strategies

questionText: A student consistently makes the same mistake in Maths despite your explanations. What do you MOST likely do next?
opt0_text: Repeat the explanation one more time, but more clearly
opt1_text: Ask the child to practice more questions of the same type
opt2_text: Give a small task to check exactly where the misconception is happening
opt3_text: Tell the parent the child needs more practice at home

questionText: Which statement do you agree with the MOST?
opt0_text: Some children are naturally better at learning than others
opt1_text: Every child can learn, but some may need different types of support
opt2_text: If students work hard, all students can reach the same level
opt3_text: Children who score well usually have higher ability

questionText: Your class has finished a chapter, but many students can’t apply the concept to real-life problems. What do you do?
opt0_text: Move ahead because syllabus completion is important
opt1_text: Give more revision worksheets for the same chapter
opt2_text: Pause and give new activities that involve using the concept in real situations
opt3_text: Ask students to memorise key points so they don’t forget

questionText: When was the last time you changed or experimented with a classroom strategy because you realised something wasn’t working?
opt1_text: In the last 1–2 months
opt3_text: I usually stick to the strategies I already know

questionText: When parents ask what they can do to support learning at home, you MOST often:
opt0_text: Give them 1–2 simple activities they can do with the child
opt1_text: Tell them to ensure the child completes homework on time
opt2_text: Say that most learning happens in school and not to worry
opt3_text: Usually do not give suggestions; parents choose their own methods
